coredata.dc:title: A highly selective chromogenic probe for the detection of nitrite in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yinyin
Zhao, Chenxi
Yue, Guizhou
Yang, Zhouping
Wang, Yanying
Rao, Hanbing
Zhang, Wei
Jin, Bo
Wang, Xianxiang
coredata.dc:description: A rapid, sensitive, and highly selective method for determining nitrite in food has been developed. This method is based on the reaction of nitrite with the amino group of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form a diazonium salt, and then the diazonium salt and glucosamine hydrochloride are coupled to each other to form an orange compound. The optimal conditions for maximum color and other analytical parameters were studied. A colorimetric method for nitrite detection has been developed with an outstanding correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9944), a wide linear range (1–75 μM) and 0.73 μM limit of detection (at S/N = 3) for nitrite ions. This method was successfully applied to the determination of nitrite in a variety of foods and gave recoveries in the range between 100.16% and 103.07%, demonstrating that the accuracy, reliability and potential application of this assay for monitoring nitrite in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Detection
Nitrite
Glucosamine hydrochloride
Colorimetric method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric determination of nitrite with TMB-GAH system
Colorimetric detection of nitrite residues in food samples
The optimum conditions for nitrite detection
Colorimetric detection of nitrite in fifteen food samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the principle of nitrite detection system.
The amino and nitrite ions of TMB form diazonium ions (a) and coupling reaction of diazonium salt with glucosamine (b).
The absorption spectra of TMB + GAH + NO2 −, TMB + GAH, TMB + NO2 − (from left to right), and GAH + NO2 −, inset is photograph of the corresponding solution.

Various concentrations of GAH solution corresponding absorbance at 451 nm (a), absorbance of different acids as solvent solution at 451 nm (b) and absorbance values at 451 nm for sodium nitrite detection system at different pH values (c).

A calibration curve between NaNO2 concentrations and absorbance values at 451 nm (inset: a picture of different sodium nitrite concentrations corresponds to solution color) (a) and absorbance at 451 nm for different TMB concentrations in a period of time (b).
Selectivity of the chromogenic probe for nitrite over negative ions (a) and metal ions (analytical wavelength: 451 nm) (b).
The results of the detection of NO2 − in the fifteen common food samples by the TMB-GAH system.

row.entry: Determination of sodium nitrite by national standard method (mg/Kg)
Determination of sodium nitrite by TMB-GAH detection system (mg/Kg)

head.ce:title: A highly selective chromogenic probe for the detection of nitrite in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid, sensitive, and highly selective method for determining nitrite in food has been developed. This method is based on the reaction of nitrite with the amino group of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form a diazonium salt, and then the diazonium salt and glucosamine hydrochloride are coupled to each other to form an orange compound. The optimal conditions for maximum color and other analytical parameters were studied. A colorimetric method for nitrite detection has been developed with an outstanding correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9944), a wide linear range (1–75 μM) and 0.73 μM limit of detection (at S/N = 3) for nitrite ions. This method was successfully applied to the determination of nitrite in a variety of foods and gave recoveries in the range between 100.16% and 103.07%, demonstrating that the accuracy, reliability and potential application of this assay for monitoring nitrite in foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is commonly known as industrial salt, which is soluble in water and slightly soluble in organic solvents such as ethanol, methanol and diethyl ether. Nitrite is widely used in food processing. For example, nitrite can be used as a coloring agent, preservative, and antibacterial agent when the concentration is appropriate, which brings benefits to the food industry (Wang et al., 2017). Nevertheless, excessive intake of nitrite is insecure and harmful to people's health (Xiang et al., 2018). Long-term consumption of foods containing nitrite reduces the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin in the blood, leading to hypoxia in the tissue and possibly lowering blood pressure or vasodilation (Fabregat, Isabel Burguete, Galindo, & Luis, 2017; Zhang et al., 2014). In addition, nitrite can also react with amines such as amino acids and phospholipids of proteins under certain circumstances and conditions to form nitrosamines (Suzuki et al., 2003). Nitrosamines have a strong carcinogenic effect and can cause harm to consumers' health. Excessive or long-term consumption of nitrite can cause harm to the human body (Wang et al., 2017) and even cause cancer, especially gastric cancer and esophageal cancer (Li, Yu, Jiang, Zhou, & Liu, 2003; Nam et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2014). Therefore, it is important to develop a simple and fast method to detect nitrite in foods.

For the past few years, multitudinous methods have been developed for the detection of nitrite in food and the environment(Dong, Zhao, Xu, & Chen, 2011; Feng, Chen, & Xu, 2015; Qian, Zhao, Xu, & Chen, 2017; Zhu, Wang, Li, & Wang, 2018)which include chromatographic analysis(Buldt & Karst, 1999; Helmke & Duncan, 2007; Jedlickova, Paluch, & Alusik, 2002; Kage, Kudo, & Ikeda, 2002), capillary isotachophoresis(Gaspar, Juhasz, & Bagyi, 2005), cyclic voltammetry(Erol, Erdogan, & Onar, 2017; Li, Liu, Wang, Mao, & You, 2017; Wang, Xu, & Chen, 2009; Xiao et al., 2018) and fluorescence spectrophotometry (Hu et al., 2019). In fact, many of the reported methods are not suitable for routine determination of nitrite in environmental samples because some of them are costly and require a professional technician. And sometimes, them are time consuming or even have low sensitivity. In recent years, significant progress has been made in the use of colorimetric detection of nitrites due to the numerous advantages of colorimetry (Amanulla et al., 2017; Li et al., 2015; Yu, Qiao, Miao, He, & Zhou, 2018; Yu, Zhang, Zhao, Xu, & Chen, 2017; Zhang, Song, Fang, & Wu, 2018). The colorimetric method is simple, rapid, highly sensitive, with small absolute error and uncomplicated instrument. It is suitable for on-site analysis and widely used in the determination of trace components and the measurement results are satisfactory. Therefore, the Ministry of Health of the People's Republic of China issued a national food safety standard for the determination of nitrite in food with colorimetric method. The principle of the determination of nitrite is that the nitrite and p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid are diazotized under the weak acid condition, and then coupled with naphthylethylenediamine hydrochloride to form a purple-red dye, and the nitrite content is measured by an external standard method. Among them, naphthylethylenediamine hydrochloride is toxic, irritating, and it is a strong carcinogenic reagent; p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid also has low toxicity. Therefore, this method for determining nitrite may cause harm to the health of operators. Nowadays, the level of technology is changing with each passing day, so new requirements are also put forward for the detection of nitrite in foods. People are paying more and more attention to the safety of reagents and the ease of operation (Nagaraja, Al-Tayar, Shivakumar, Shrestha, & Gowda, 2010). TMB as an ideal chromophore, it has some applications in colorimetric test. In this case, Serrat F.B. has reported a method for determining residual chlorine in water using TMB. The principle is that under acidic conditions (PH ≤ 2), TMB reacts with residual chlorine to form yellow quinone compound, namely TMB diimine salt. Using TMB's color development principle, it can be used as a new and safe chromogen reagent for peroxidase to determine residual chlorine and nitrite in drinking water (Serrat, 1994). Zhang has developed a method that rapidly oxidizing TMB to golden yellow by reacting nitrous acid with hydrogen peroxide to form peroxynitrite and the sodium nitrite in water was measured (Zhang, Yang, Chen, & Yang, 2013). Unfortunately, hydrogen peroxide used in this method is an explosive and strong oxidant that threatens the health of laboratory operators (Ibrahim et al., 2019).

The aim of this work is to quantify the concentration of nitrite using TMB-glucosamine hydrochloride detection system. Glucosamine hydrochloride (GAH) is a safety biological product that is produced naturally in humans, and it has application as joint medicine. Under the acidic conditions, the nitrite, GAH and TMB can form an orange substance, and the color shade is related to nitrite concentration. This method is simple, safe, economical and has already been used successfully for detecting nitrite in some food samples.

All chemicals were analytical grade. Glucosamine hydrochloride (GAH) was purchased from Jinan Haidebei Marine Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), obtained from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals, including sodium nitrite and other inorganic salts or acid, were of analytical grade and could be used from Chengdu Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. without further purification. All solutions were prepared by using double distilled water. All food samples were obtained from the local supermarket. UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded on an Aoyi A-390 spectrometer with 1.0 cm path length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric determination of nitrite with TMB-GAH system
ce:section.ce:para: In a procedure for the preparation of acidic GAH solution, 0.05 g GAH was dissolved in 50 mL, 18% acetic acid (the final concentration is 1 mg/mL), then the solution was stirred for some times until the GAH was dissolved completely, and the prepared solution was stored at 4 °C in refrigerator. Next, 0.04 g TMB was dissolved in 10 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (the final concentration is 16.64 μM). Next, 0.5 mL of TMB solution was added with 0.5 mL acidic GAH (1 mg/mL, pH = 2) to form TMB-GAH detection system. After homogeneous mixing, various concentrations of nitrite solutions (including 75, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10, 7.5, 5, 2.5, 1 μM) were spiked to the TMB-GAH system. Qualitatively check the color change of the solution from blank to orange by the naked eye, or quantitatively measure with a spectrophotometer. An absorbance spectrum was acquired as an average of several times independent measurements. The intensity of the absorbance values was recorded to establish a linear calibration curve. The response of TMB-GAH system to other ions was also investigated to verifythe specificity for nitrite detection.

In our preliminary experiment, we discussed the effects of pH, the concentration of GAH and the types of acid on the sensitivity of the system response by single-factor test, and finally the optimum preparation conditions of the system were obtained. Different concentrations of GAH (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 mg/mL) were discussed to select the optimal concentration of GAH. And GAH (50 mg) was dissolved in different kinds of acids, including formic acid, acetic acid, propionic acid, hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, sulfuric acid and nitric acid, and then make them up to 50 mL. They were used for selecting the best kind of acid in the system. Different pH values (2, 2.5, 3 and 3.5) were investigated in presence of 1 mg/mL GAH dissolved in acetic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of nitrite residues in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: 10 food categories such as egg products, dairy products and many more, including 15 food samples were obtained from local market (Chengdu, China). The first is the processing sample. We used the quartering method to take the right amount of food samples and then make it into a homogenate. Next, we extracted nitrite. 5.00 g sample was made into a homogenate sample (if water is added during the preparation process, it should be converted according to the amount of water added) and placed in a beaker (50 mL). Then, a saturated borax solution (12.5 mL) was added and stirred well (the final concentration is 50 g/L). The sample was washed into a beaker at about 70 °C (about 300 mL) and heated in a boiling water bath for 15 min. It was taken out and cooled in a cold water bath and then placed at room temperature. Finally, the extract solution was purified. The resulting sample solution was stored at 4 °C for the next test. While shaking the above extract, potassium iron cyanide solution (5 mL, 106 g/L) was added, shake well, then zinc acetate solution (5 mL, 220 g/L) was added to precipitate protein. Next, water was added to a volume of 500 mL, shaken, placed for 30 min, the upper layer of fat was removed, the supernatant was filtered with filter paper, and the first filtrate was discarded 30 mL. The filtrate was ready for measuring (Li, Ma, Duan, Deng, & Li, 2018).

In this study, a nitrite sensor has been constructed using TMB and acidic GAH. The change of color is measured for quantitative analysis of nitrite ions. The working principle of our strategy is shown in Fig. 1 . In fact, the TMB solution, the sodium nitrite solution and the GAH solution are all colorless and transparent. Above all, we mixed the TMB solution and the sodium nitrite solution, and then the mixed solution immediately turns orange when the GAH solution is added.

At the presence of nitrite, the color of the TMB-GAH system is changed by chemical reaction potentially. We have hypothesized that the reaction works as follows: Under strong acid conditions, nitrite reacts with the amino group of TMB to form diazonium salt (Fig. 2 a) (Colman, 2010; Zheng et al., 2018). Then, the diazonium salt ion and the GAH are coupled to each other to form an orange macroscopic compound (Fig. 2b).

We have selected TMB-GAH system as the detection system. Fig. 3 shows that when the three components are present at the same time, the absorbance at 451 nm of the reaction system is obviously the highest, and a bright orange solution could be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimum conditions for nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of GAH, the kind of acids and the pH of the solution affect the reaction. Fig. 4 a shows that the various concentrations of GAH (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 mg/mL) had inconspicuous influences on the sensibility of the system. Meanwhile, the pH regulated by the concentration of acetic acid and the kinds of acid had significant influences on the reaction.

Fig. 4b reveals that small molecule organic acids, including formic acid, acetic acid and propionic acid, can be used as solvent of GAH to prepare nitrite detection system, while hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, sulfuric acid, nitric acid and other inorganic strong acids cannot. Fig. 4c indicates that pH of the system has a significant impact on the detection of nitrite ions. We have observed that the lower pH of the system, the more sensitive to the detection of nitrite ions. When the pH is raised to 3.5, the UV absorption peak of the solution at 451 nm disappeared.

In sum, we have concluded that the optimal nitrite detection system conditions are the concentration of GAH is 1 mg/mL, and the solvent is acetic acid, and the pH is 2. Under such an optimal condition, the purpose of quickly and accurately testing the concentration of nitrite can be achieved.

The 0.5 mL stock TMB solution (16.64 μM) has been added into 0.5 mL stock acidic GAH solution (1 mg/mL, pH = 2). After homogeneous mixing, different concentrations of nitrite, including 75, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10, 7.5, 5, 2.5, 1 μM, were spiked to the mixed solutions respectively. The developing color change of solution from blank to orange is qualitatively inspected by the naked eye or quantitatively measured with a spectrophotometer.

With the increase of NO2 − concentration, the absorbance value of the solution increased. Excellent linearity between nitrite concentrations (1–75 μM) and absorbance values at 451 nm is observed with correlation coefficient of 0.9944 (inset in Fig. 5 a). The linear equation is Y = 0.01327X-0.0176 (X and Y represent the NaNO2 concentration (μM) and absorbance value at 451 nm, respectively). And the results show that the limit of detection (LOD) of this method is 0.73 μM. Fig. 5a indicates that the color of solution is enhanced with increasing concentration of NO2 −. The addition of sodium nitrite (0, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10 μM) does not show the change in color of the solution by the naked eye. But as the higher concentration of sodium nitrite is added (20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 75 μM), the color of the detection system gradually deepens. Actually, absorbance values of solution are enhanced with increasing concentration of NO2 − from 1 μM to 75 μM at 451 nm. Fig. 5b reveals that with the increase of TMB concentration, we can clearly observe that the sensitivity of the system detection is improved, and the absorbance at 451 nm is also increased immediately.

To test the specificity of the TMB-GAH system, 75 μM of metal ions which include NaNO2, MgCl2, FeCl3, CaCl2, KCl, AlCl3, ZnCl2, SnCl2 and 75 μM of negative ions which include NaHCO3, Na2SO4, NaH2PO4, Na2B4O7, NaCl, K2HPO4, NaNO3, Na2MoO4, Na2SO3 are both prepared in double distilled water and then add them into TMB-GAH system.

From Fig. 6 we can clearly find that there is no change in color with non-specific ions (including some metal ions and some negative ions), while a significant change only in color is observed with the present of nitrite. This result demonstrates that TMB-GAH detection system is only selective for nitrite and not sensitive for other ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of nitrite in fifteen food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrite residue in fifteen food samples were determined by the Griess reagent and TMB-GAH system, and the test results were compared in this work. The unknown concentration is calculated by placing absorbance value into the corresponding calibration curve. The level of nitrite residue (NO2 −) in the fifteen food samples detected by Griess-based colorimetry is between 0.500 and 7.448 mg/kg. In order to evaluate the universality and uniqueness of the method, it was applied for the detection of nitrite in fifteen food samples which involved ten varieties of common foods. Determination of nitrite content in food samples by spike recovery, and the results were listed in Table 1 . By using the TMB-GAH system, the recovery range from 100.16% to 103.07% and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) values are less than 4.0%. What’s more, the detection results from the current experiment is consistent with those measured by Griess-based colorimetric method, the relative error of the two methods is less than 7%. It indicates that this method is suitable for the quantification of nitrite in a variety of foods, including dairy products, egg products, biscuits, etc.

In the current work, we report a method for rapid detection of nitrite in multiple food samples using the TMB-GAH detection system. The nitrite concentration has a good linear relationship with the absorbance at 451 nm(R2 = 0.9944) , a wide linear range (1–75 μM), and the LOD is 0.73 μM. The TMB-GAH detection system has the advantages of sensitive detection, good selectivity, safe reagents and simple operation. In addition, the nitrite detection method proposed in this work is consistent with the performance of Griess-based method for detecting nitrite in fifteen food samples (relative error <7%).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305097), General Project of Education Department in Sichan (Project No. 035Z2270) and the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 00770113).
